description: Apollo 16 Flight Journal - Day 1, Part 4: Transposition, Docking and Ejection

tokens: Apollo 16 has completed the Translunar Injection burn (TLI) of the S-IVB third stage of the Saturn V booster.

The S-IVB now holds only 2.75 tonnes of the 108.45 tonnes of propellant that it was launched with, the balance having been consumed to place the stage and the attached Apollo spacecraft on the long, three day ballistic coast to the Moon.

The 40-metre long, 65-tonne 'stack' of booster and spacecraft is travelling at 36,360 kilometres per hour, but is slowing down rapidly since it is still subject to approximately one quarter of Earth's surface gravity.
It will continue to slow down until the Moon's gravitational pull exceeds that of the Earth, in about two days time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Inside the Command Module, John Young, Ken Mattingly and Charlie Duke are continuing to prepare to separate the Command and Service Modules (CSM) from the conical shroud known as the SLA, in 20 minutes time.

(NASA sources of the time give varying meanings for this acronym; either Service Module/Lunar Module Adapter as in the Flight Plan or, more commonly, Spacecraft/Lunar Module Adapter.) This will then allow them to turn the CSM around, dock with the Lunar Module (LM) and extract it from the SLA.
This is referred to as Transposition, Docking and Extraction (TD&E)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At present, the on-board tape recorder is not selected on, so there is no transcript of the crew's discussions.
The CM transcript will start at003:03:09, two minutes before separation.
For now, we can only follow the crew's communications with Mission Control, where the CapCom is Gordon Fullerton, just as it has been through launch, Earth orbit and the TLI burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston.
I'm going to Omni Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: See 001:29:49 for a description of the four omni-directional S-Band antennae the crew are using to maintain communication with Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Gordy, you got Omni Charlie.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger; Omni Charlie, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 2 hours, 51 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our countdown clock at Mission Control shows the time of separation is less than 13 minutes away now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, at 2 hours, 54 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Coming up now on that time when the booster initiates its maneuver to separation attitude.
We presently show Apollo 16 at 1,974 nautical miles [3,656 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: After the TLI burn, at 002:41:50, the S-IVB and attached Apollo spacecraft was manoeuvred to the in-plane local horizontal.
This involved a -25 degree pitch (down), and minus 1 degree yaw (left).
Now, at 002:54:19, the stack is being commanded to manouevre +120 degrees (up), -40 degrees (yaw) and -180 degrees in roll.
This will give the crew the optimum lighting conditions for the TD&E.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, and we are maneuvering to the attitude right now.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: 16, we see the cabin is up to 5.7 now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: In Mission Control, the EECOM is monitoring the cabin pressure, which has now reached the target of 5.7 pounds per square inch, gauge [39.3 kPa].
The crew will now close the Direct O2valve.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Thank you, sir.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We'd like Omni Delta, please.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Omni Alpha now, please.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Say again.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Give us Omni Alpha, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, you got it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Request Omni Bravo now, please.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You got it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Seven minutes away now from time of separation.
We presently show Apollo 16 at an altitude of 2,620 nautical miles [4,852 km].
We are at 2 hours, 58 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Flight director Gene Kranz, is taking a check with his flight control team, for a Go/No-Go for transposition, docking and ejection of the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Like Omni Charlie, please.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Roger; Omni Charlie.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, at 2 hours 59 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We presently show Apollo 16 at 2,800 nautical miles [5,185 km] and velocity at 26,931 feet per second [8,208 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 16, Houston.
The booster is in attitude and stable.
You have a Go for T&D.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The S-IVB has completed its manoeuvre to the TD&E attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.] We'll give you a call just before we get off.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 3 hours Ground Elapsed Time.
Present altitude of Apollo 16, 3,004 nautical miles [5,563 km].
Velocity now reading at 26,408 feet [8,049 metres] per second.
We're four minutes away now from time of - proposed time of separation.
This is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, at 3 hours, 2 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 16's present distance away from Earth now 3,388 nautical miles [6,274 kilometres].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Houston.
We're getting ready to arm the pyros.
Are you ready?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We're ready.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay; Pyro Arm A is Armed; and B is Armed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: CM Transcript starts.
The crew are on AOH Page 4-322, working through the TD&E checklist.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.] That looks good.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, at 3 hours, 4 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 16's presently at a height of 3,703 nautical miles [6,858 kilometres].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 3 hours, 4 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 16 presently at a height of 3,703 nautical miles [6,858 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, we're coming up on 59:40.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Mark.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Separation of the CSM from the SLA is a fast but complex event.

A train of explosive cords sever electrical connections between the Service Module and the S-IVB; they cut the metal structure joining the SM to the SLA to allow the spacecraft to come free; they cut the upper 75% of the conical SLA into four long sections which are now only joined to the S-IVB by spring loaded partial hinges at the centre of their lower edge; they set off pyrotechnic thrusters, mounted within the intact portion of the SLA, which force pistons to push on the outside edge of each SLA panel, causing them to begin rotating away from the enclosed Lunar Module.

Once the panels have rotated about 45° from the centreline of the launch vehicle, the hinges disengage, allowing the springs within the hinge assembly to push the panels away at about 2.5 m/s, leaving the LM exposed on top of the Saturn's third stage.
(A15FJ)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 3 hours, 5 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've had small sep[aration] burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The crew are now working through Serial 5, placing the CSM in required post-separation configuration (AOH Vol 2 Page 4-325)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Checking that all SM RCS propellant valves and helium supply valves are closed.

It's pitching around now.

tokens: Roger, John.

We see the particles and - great picture!

She looks great.

tokens: Yeah.

transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Houston.
We're hard docked.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, John.
We saw it come in.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yep.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, Ken.
[Long pause.]

tokens: 3 hours, 22 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 16 at 6,845 nautical miles [12,677 km] away.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: John, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Go ahead.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'd like - We noticed the mixing valves cycling about once every 10, 15 seconds.
We'd like to give you a mark, at which time we want you to put the Glycol Evap Temp In Valve in Manual and try to catch the flow rate at a - at a appropriate setting.
I'll - I'll give you kind of a countdown and a mark here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, we've been noticing that ourselves.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, flow rate's high.
It's starting back down.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: John, now all of a sudden we've stopped seeing it - that it has stopped cycling.
You didn't throw the switch already, did you?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's negative.
We're awaiting your mark there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, it - (chuckle) it just hung up.
Just as I said that.
Stand by.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like you to cycle the Manual back to Auto and see if that starts it back down.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, you were in Manual for about two seconds and back to Auto.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.] Okay, it's coming down now.
Stand by to put it in Manual.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ready.
Now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
Okay, you got it right there on the "now." [Pause.](onboard): Okay; say that again, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I was a little slow on the "now," but leave it there for now and press on with the normal procedures.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: [Laughter.]
transcript.speaker: Crew

tokens: CM Transcript ends.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, before we turn the TV off, we gon - we want to give you a picture of the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, I'll appreciate that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

That view of Earth from a distance of 7,500 nautical miles [13,890 kilometres].

We can see Southwestern United States, Lower California.
Very nice.

tokens: Good.
Ken's doing all that good work for you.
It's out his window.

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

Beautiful color.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

See the reds in the desert down there and the Southern United States and northern part of Mexico.
And from here, you see the Great Lakes and the State of Florida out there.
And it's just absolutely something.
We're going to go back to work, but thought you'd enjoy that.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Thank you for the picture.
It's the next best thing to being up there.
[Pause.]

We'll extend the time on commercial TV lines here if - if it looks like we'll get some good shots on the S-IVB.

tokens: Okay.

tokens: Roger; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Gordy.
So when we pitched around, I'd like to tell you a little bit about something we saw on the LM.
When we were coming around, out - about 30 or 40 feet [9 to 12 metres] out, we had a lot of white particles.
Looked like it was coming out from around the Lunar Module.
Quite a number of them.
And, as we got closer, it looked like to me that the primary - most of the particles were coming bet - from between the ascent propellant tank over quad one and this omni antenna.
And it looks like they was being jetted out from either some outgassing or something, and we assumed it's Mylar but are not convinced of that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We copy that, Charlie.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The only reason we comment on it, it just seemed like there was a awful lot of them.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Long comm break.

tokens: Gordy, we've - we lost the - the monitor picture, and we're gonna try to power the TV set down.
We're gonna check all the connections, and it's got a lot of horizontal lines.
And you really can't even make out the image.
It started out okay, and then while John was taking a picture, it - the monitor picture went out.
So we're gonna try to take a look at it.
It's got a whole bunch of horizontal lines.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, we'll go to...

tokens: It looks like maybe multiple images.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

We now show Apollo 16 at a distance of 14,416 nautical miles [26,698 km].

tokens: 16, Houston.
The maneuver is complete.
We're standing by for your Go for the evasive burn.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, stand by one.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Gordy, we're all set.
It looks like it's almost 90 degrees to us.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.

On the TV problem, we had a good picture out at the site there at the first, but then we started losing signal strength, which would - doesn't really tell us whether anything is wrong with your monitor set or not.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
Well, we're gonna take pictures like it's working, and you can check it out later.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And we are all set.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 16 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Booster Systems engineer reports he will start with the evasive burn of the S-IVB at 4 hours, 18 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 16, Houston.
Goldstone says they're getting a good picture, and so your trouble is worth the effort there, and we'll start the evasive burn at 4:18 even.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Gordy, thank you.
We got another spectacular view of the Earth down here.
The polar ice cap.
We can see the whole sphere, and the United States is absolutely spectacular.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: How about that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And out the other side, we've got a crescent Moon.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: In fact, you can see Lake Mead, Gordy; very clearly.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: No kidding?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 17 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 16 now 15,039 nautical miles [27,852 km] away from the Earth.
Velocity now reading 15,272 feet per second [4,655 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 18 minutes.
Booster Systems engineer indicates he has initiated the evasive burn.
That's 1 minute, 20 seconds in duration.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We can see her moving away now, Gordon, and she's just slowly picking up a little speed there.
Only way you can tell it's moving is against the - the particles in the background.
I don't think you can see those on TV, but it's - it looks like there's a million stars out behind the S-IVB as it moves off.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, John.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Now the evasive burn is complete, now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And - Roger.
And as she moves out of sight, the old Apollo 16 crew would really like to express their thanks and appreciation to the guys at the Marshall Space Flight Center that gave such a phenomenal ride.
Not to mention the Boeing Company on the First Stage, North American on the Second, McDAC on the Third, IBM on the IU.
It was superb all the way.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, John, I'll speak to them.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 20 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
That was spacecraft commander John Young expressing his appreciation of the Saturn V team.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And you might relay to old Mike Wash, thanks a lot for his help.
We know he's leaving, and we're sure glad we didn't have to use any of that training he gave us.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, we'll sure do that.
He's just about to walk out the door.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: 4 hours, 21 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Mike Wash has worked with the Apollo flight crews in the - in training these crews for the powered phase of launch and also...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Gordy, up off - looks like Alaska - up maybe a little farther north - is a pretty good swirl pattern.
Looks like a pretty good storm up there.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: During the powered phase of flight, Mike Wash is always positioned right next to the Capsule Communicator.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 16, here is a word from the auxiliary CapCom here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Good luck, you fellows.
Take it easy, and hope everything works out all right.
transcript.speaker: Wash

tokens: Kind wordks, Mike.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Say again?
transcript.speaker: Wash

tokens: Thank you for all your trouble.
We sure enjoyed working with you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, was sure my pleasure, John; thank you a lot.
Good-bye and good luck.
transcript.speaker: Wash

tokens: Thank you, now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 22 minutes.
The voice you just heard was Mike Wash who is being transferred to the Ames Research Center.
We're at 4 hours, 23 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We show Apollo 16 at a distance of 15,707 nautical miles [29,089 km] away from the Earth.
Velocity now reading 14,972 feet per second [4,563 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay and the - the S-IVB has drifted maybe half a mile away now, so we went ahead and turned off the tube.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: John, just before you turned the mon - the TV off, was the monitor still giving you trouble?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I guess it's about time for a little Verb 49 to the P52 attitude.
How's that suit you?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Sounds good.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, the - I can't get over the view of that Earth.
None of the pictures just do it justice.
Absolutely beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're kind of getting the idea that you're impressed.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Man, the thing about it, Gordy, is that the whole Southern United States, Mexico, and that - and - and Florida and Cuba and the Virgin Islands down that way - they're all clear of clouds.
It's just fantastic!
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Did you take some good pictures?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Got some.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The way we're going, we may have to get a reload before we get to the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: As a matter of fact, you can see as far north as Lake Michigan and Lake Superior.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Sounds great.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And all the way down past the Yucatan and - and into the Central America.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we've got the cabin back up some now, and our LM/CM Delta-P gauge is reading 0.6, and that's probably due to that Delta[-P] on the cabin.
And the O2Flow Hi light has gone out, so things are getting back to normal.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Houston, we've done a LM/CM Delta-P; and at time four hours and 30 minutes, we had a plus 0.6.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we're going to take the Waste Stowage valve to Vent [at] this time.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Break in CM transcript until 004:39:52
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, at 4 hours, 37 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our space digitals display in Mission Control [is] presently using the Moon as a reference and we show that Apollo 16 is 174,639 nautical miles [323,431 kilometres] away from the Moon.
At 4 hours, 37 minutes, continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Ah, 16, Houston.
Don't know if you can see it or not, but we've started the LOX dump on the S-IVB.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We lost it a little while ago, Gordy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 40 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
The Booster Systems engineer reports that the LOX dump has been completed with the S-IVB.
We're at 4 hours, 41 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, are you ready for us to start charging Battery B?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, we're Go for the battery charge on B, and we'd also like you to dial in the Flight Plan High Gain angles, minus 47 and 98, and go to Reacq[uire].
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, you got the angles, and you're going to Reacq[uire].
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Mark.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 16.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we got Bat B charging, and it says volts should be 37½ to 39½, and I'm looking at 33.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Charlie, we got - we figured about 8 amp-hours out of that battery, so it'll be a while yet before the voltage gets back up, and EECOM thinks that's okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, fine.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, we got 3.4 on 7A.
We're gonna vent - we gonna vent the battery to zero, if that's okay.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Stand by.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like you to hold on that a minute.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You're too late, we just vented it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, it's reading about two-tenths right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Say again, four-tenths.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Four-tenths.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 50 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
The space digitals display is still using the Moon as a reference.
We presently show Apollo 16 at a distance of 172 - 173,082 nautical miles [320,548 km] away from the Moon.
Continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, got a couple of comments on the EMS for G&C, whenever he has a break.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: They're all ears; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That must look funny.
Okay.
We been running null bias tests here for - well, since we got in orbit - each time the checklist calls for it.
On the first one, we found that in our hundred-second check, it gained 2.5; then, just before docking, it got 2.6; and I just ran another one, and I have 2.8.
And I don't really know what you can do with that, but I just thought I'd go ahead and tell you about that's the magnitude of what we're looking at.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, and our first rough guess as to how the SCS GDC system is performing, it looks like it's well within spec in pitch, yaw, and roll, as far as its drift measurements go.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Ken and John, we got that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: 16, Houston.
Whenever you're ready, we're ready to load the PTC REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, you have P00 and Accept.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ken, sorry about that.
We didn't get coordinated here.
We don't have an uplink site, so go back to Block until after 5 hours, and we'll try it again then.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: All righty.
We're back in UpTel to Block.
And we're going to be kind of hanging up here for a while anyhow, while we get our suits off.
It - it turns out to be a pretty interesting operation with these new B-suits.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'll tell you, Gordy, there were some sights out there that were really something.
One of the - one of the things that - most things happened like people had said they would.
But there were a couple of things that I had never seen or heard anyone even mention, and maybe they'd been on - been there all along.

But one of the things that was really nifty was, while we were in powered flight, both in later stages of the boost and - and during the TLI burn, there were particles that I could see out the window that were going past us in the plus-X direction, and I kept thinking that that was an optical illusion, and I kept going back and looking at it again, and sure enough!
And these were after we were in steady state.
It wasn't around any kind of a staging event or anything that I was aware of.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Oh, how about that.
I haven't heard of that one before.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Then when we - when we scooted out here and you started your nonpropulsive vent, we could see - First, it just looked like - like it was a little mist around the outside when you looked at the Sun.
And then the Sun hit it at such an angle that you started getting a - a spectral reflection, and it looked like a rainbow out over the LM.

And then after - after that, you could look out my Number 1 window, and apparently the lighting was just right so that it - it had the appearance of - of light streaming off to a point source at infinity.
There was a little blank spot in it at the - at what looked like the origin.
And then all these streaks were coming back towards you, like you were right in the center of a cone.
And these things would change colors.
They go to a light - light purple, and then they'd have a little sandy color to them.
That was another one that I don't remember ever hearing before, and it was - maybe it was just the lighting on it, but it sure was pretty.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Rog.
Enjoying the description.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'll tell you, you can't wait too many years to make this worth it.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, on that boost, that S-IC is a real freight train.
I'll tell you, boy.
I can't get over that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Could you folks see that thing on the TV up through staging?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I didn't watch it all the way until it went out of sight.
I'll have to check here.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Yeah, I guess we saw even [the LES] tower jett.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Good show.
John's in the middle of his suit doff.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're at 4 hours, 58 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Most of that long description coming from Ken Mattingly, how...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And thanks to good old Stu Roosa's suggestion, we wrote in the Flight Plan to be sure and take a look at the fires out there in Africa as we went over, which is something we probably would have forgotten or never even thought about.
And they are just as beautiful as everybody's ever said they could be.
They're just all over the place.
All these little - little yellowish-red dots down there.
And there was some - looked like some low overcast in parts of the area, or maybe it was - from our altitude, maybe it was a high overcast.
But it just gave - It looked like looking at the lights of a city through fog, and then there were others that were clear.
Just something well worth remembering to look for.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Roger.
We'll be sure to remind Ron to look for that one.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I tell you, God didn't equip us with enough eyes to see everything that there is to see in the first hour.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Rog.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 4 hours, 59 minutes.
That last remark coming from Command Module Pilot Ken Mattingly.
Our space digital display still using the Moon as a reference.
We show Apollo 16 172,048 nautical miles [318,633 kilometres] away from the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Gordy, it looks like this whole operation may take us longer than - than we had guessed.
Is there any thermal constraint on getting a 52 attitude and going to those other attitudes?
Looks like we got some time to go here...
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I'll check on that, Ken.
We're about...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: ...but I just wondered if that's something we ought to keep in mind.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay.
We're about to a handover here.
I'll check on that and come back to you through Hawaii.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: 16, we're - through Hawaii now.
And you're scheduled in this attitude through 7 hours or - at least, so no problem thermally, and you're not even due to do the P52 for another half hour, so you're plenty ahead.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, that P52 - We're going to come to a decision point; here pretty soon, whether to - you want us to do that and then we'll pick up the suit doffing after that, or we - I'd just as soon go ahead and do the - get all the suits out of the way.
And we can do the 52 on schedule, or we can do it after we get the suits off.
Does it make any difference to you folks ?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Let me check.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If that's what you'd like to do, why don't you go ahead with the - finish up the suits.
No problem slipping the 52 a little bit.
And we'd like P00 and Accept for that uplink.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, there's P00 and Accept.
[Pause.] Is Fredo still around there?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, he went home about a half hour ago.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 16, Houston.
The computer is yours.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 5 hours, 10 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our displays still show the Moon as our reference.
We show Apollo 16 at a distance of 170,878 nautical miles [316,466 km] away from the Moon and we've had no conversation for a while with the crew of Apollo 16.
We suspect that they're in the process of doffing their space suits and later preparation for their eat period which is scheduled to begin at six hours Ground Elapsed Time.
Five hours, 10 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 5 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our space digital display now using the Earth reference.
We show Apollo 16 at 23,892 nautical miles [44,248 km] away from the Earth.
Velocity now reading 12,295 feet per second [3,747 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 5 hours, 44 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We presently show Apollo 16 at a distance of 25,488 nautical miles [46,204 km] away from the Earth.
Velocity now reading 11,945 feet per second [3,504 m/s].
Very shortly in Mission Control, we will have a change of shift - or shift changeover of the Gene Kranz team of flight controllers will be replaced by Pete Frank's team of flight controllers.
We're at 5 hours, 45 minutes and continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 5 hours, 50 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We presently show Apollo 16 at a distance of 26,143 nautical miles [48,417 km] away from the Earth.
And traveling at a velocity of 11,796 feet per second [3,595 m/s].
We're continuing with our shift turnover in the Mission Control Center at the present time.
Pete Frank's team of Orange flight controllers coming aboard, replacing the Gene Kranz team of White flight controllers.
We estimate the start time of our change of shift news conference at 6 to 6:15 pm Central Standard Time.
The news conference will involve Flight Director Gene Kranz, CapCom astronaut Gordon Fullerton and Booster Systems engineer Frank Van Rensselaer.
This news conference will be held in the News Center briefing auditorium - in the News Center briefing auditorium instead of the large public affairs auditorium.
We're at 5 hours, 51 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hello, Houston; 16.
John and I are back up now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
That up-link is complete.
In case you didn't hear me some time ago, the computer is yours.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we're in Block.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And it took us about an hour for me and Charlie to climb out of those suits and stow them.
It's really something.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Rog.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Removal and stowage of the A-7LB spacesuits was discussed by John Young, Ken Mattingly and Charlie Duke during the 1972 post-mission debrief:
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"Climbing out of that suit is really something."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"We had a hard time getting the suits into the suit bag because we were trying to be careful of them.
I don't know if taking our pockets off would have helped us or not."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"Taking the pockets off is another 10 or 15 minutes per suit.
The way they put those things on they're on there to stay.
The best way to get them off is to cut them off."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"The only point I'm making is that I have stowed suits in the bag in stowage exercises and it's a not a big deal."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"It's entirely different in flight and I think there are couple of things that are different.
One is the suits which we stowed here [on Earth] never had the pockets and all the paraphenalia straps on.
The other thing is you have an old beat up suit that's a rag.
You don't care if you step on it, kick it, or what you do to it but when you re on your way to the Moon, you're being ginger with those things."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"The bag is just too small for the suits."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"Here's something I feel pretty strong about.

We haven't had any problems so far but I think that the crews that are going to have to take these suits off and stow them in a bag should be given a demonstration on how to properly roll up and stow an A-7LB pressure suit by a suit technician close to launch so they don't forget what they've learned.
Course, that's just another square to fill close to launch.

If you damage that suit by stowing it in that bag, and I can see how you could do that because you wouldn't believe the kind of kicking, grunting, shoving and heaving you have to do in zero g to get that suit in there, and if you damage it, there goes your mission because you can't fix it."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"I think that you should point out, we only stowed two suits in a three-suit bag."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"Yes.
We only put two suits in there.
We got three suits in there on the way back and then found out that we weren't supposed to have three suits in there.
We were supposed to stow one of them under the couch.
And, we only put two in there on the way out.
We stowed Ken's suit undernearth the left couch.
But, I was always concerned that maybe we had folded a suit in the wrong direction and put some undue strain on the zipper.
We had zipped the zipper up, but we hadn't zipped the pressure sealing zipper up all the way.
But we zipped the restraint zipper all the way up and we put the neck ring's dust cover on it."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"I think it's a shame to take a chance on the suits on your way to the Moon.
I think you should make that bag bigger."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"There's about this much room between the bag and the front hatch which is not used and I don't see why they don't make that big enough so that a guy is not ruining his EVA by stowing his pressure suit.
Now, maybe the CSD people will come back and say you can't hurt those pressure suits.
But if I know Ed Smiley, I reckon he feels that way about it, probably."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"Well, the other thing is, when you fold these on the ground and you got one g helping you pull it up into a little ball.
In flight, it is whatever you could get with your arms."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1972 Technical debrief:"The other problem is that J-mission spacecraft, with those boxes under you, only one man can get in there to do the job at any one time,You can't get two men in the space to push the suit right.

Well, it was of some concern to me in that we were treating suits properly when we stowed them that first time.We set a new world record for suit donning and doffing in zero gravity and 1/6-gravity seven times, something I would just as soon not have,we were behind the first day.

We didn't have enough time in the timeline to doff the suits and stow them, we didn't have any time in there with one man in the middle of suit doffing, another man is handicapped because he's helping.
So, that leaves one man to mind the store."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mattingly, from the1972 Technical debrief:"We actually had time in the Flight Plan for doffing the suits.
We just didn't have adequate time.
We had an hour or less and we used almost one hour on the first suit."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 16, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Go ahead, Gordon.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm going to hand over to Pete here.
He's coming on with a good boost there.
He's got a bunch of - P37 PAD and a bunch of Flight Plan updates for you when you get somebody free to do some stenographic work there.
Enjoyed the first six hours.
Hope the rest of it goes as well.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordon, that was beautiful.
Tell Flight and the guys down in the trenches, "Man, that was super!"
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: At this point there is a shift change in Mission Control, with the Orange Team under Pete Frank taking over control.
The new CapCom is Donald Peterson.
"Pete" Peterson joined the astronaut corps in 1969, but did not fly until the sixth Shuttle mission in 1983.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston; 16 here.
We're ready to copy the Flight Plan updates.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger, 16.
At 11 hours in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Stand by.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
At 11 hours, we want to delete "Waste Stowage Vent valve, Close;" and, at 12:15, we will add "Waste Stowage Vent valve, Close." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; copy add at 12:15 "Waste Stowage Vent valve, Close."
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
And delete it at 11.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Then we've got the change to the CSM Experiments/EVA Checklist having to do with the ultraviolet filter.
[Pause.] UV filter apparently did not meet the specs, and we're going to have to make some changes to the exposures at - on several different pages in the checklist.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: We'll wait on that; we don't have that checklist out yet, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; and I've got P37 block data.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Pete, go ahead with the P37 block data.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Lift-off plus 15.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Roger; copy.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Rog.
That's correct, Johnny.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Judging by the voice from the spacecraft, Peterson is almost certainly talking with Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Is that all?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah [garble] that's all.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
How's the midcourse [correction] looking?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stand by one.
We're still looking at it; it looks pretty good right now.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We've now completed our shift handover in Mission Control.
Flight Director Pete Frank and the Orange Team of Flight Controllers.
The CapCom on this shift is astronaut Donald Peterson.

We have a change of shift press briefing scheduled to begin momentarily in the MSC News Center briefing room as is our normal practice during change of shift briefings, we will have the air to ground line down and we'll be recording any conservations with the crew for playback following the press conference.
The coming eight hours or so should be a relatively quiet time for the crew.
The booster engineer has pretty well taken care of all activities with the Saturn V third stage, the S-IVB.
That vehicle is now gradually separating from the spacecraft [and] tumbling slowly.

This is to maintain the proper thermal equilibrium and also to kind of neutralize out any changes in velocity added or subtracted by small ventings from any of the tanks, a normal procedure with the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The crew is scheduled to begin a eat period and as we passed up to Charlie Duke we do not expect to have Midcourse Correction 1 based on the current tracking data.
At 6 hours, 19 minutes; Apollo 16 is traveling at a velocity of 11,159 feet per second [3,401 m/s].
Now 29,187 nautical miles [54,054 km] from Earth.
This is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, now have Noun 93; are you happy?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: In a minute, 16.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, 16.
You can go ahead and program.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: 16, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Just wanted to remind you that, before you start the UV photography, we've got to change all the exposures.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 6 hours, 41 minutes.
During the change of shift briefing, we accumulated a small amount of tape conversations which we'll play back for you at this time.
And then continue to stand by live following that tape playback.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Pete, 16 here.
Go ahead with the update.
Give me a page number for the UV, and we'll update the filter settings.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; we've got a whole bunch of pages.
We'll start on 2-16.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Under item 4, we want to change from "20 seconds - 2 frames" to "2 seconds - 2 frames." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; keep going.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; on page 2-17.
Item 4, "20 seconds - 2 frames," change to "1/15 second - 2 frames." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Page 2...
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; 2-16 - 2-16, line 4, "20 seconds" to "2 seconds," and 2-17, same line is "1/15" versus "20 seconds."
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's affirmative.
And on page 2-19, we want to change shutter - under item 5 - we want to change "Shutter 1/15 - 2 frames" to "Shutter 1 - 2 frames." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; "Shutter 1/15" went to "Shutter 1 - 2 frames."
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
On page 2-21, we have change from "Shutter 1/15 - 2 frames," to "Shutter 1 - 2 frames."
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
That's item 2?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Affirmative; that's item 2.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay; that was "Shutter 1" vice "1/15."
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Affirmative.
And on page 2-22, item 2, change "Shutter 1/15 - 2 frames" to "Shutter 1/2 - 2 frames." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Copy; 1/2.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On page 2-23, item 4, "20 seconds - 2 frames" to "2 seconds - 2 frames." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And on page 2-24, under "At T start plus 7 minutes", change "20 seconds - 2 frames" to "1/15 seconds - 2 frames."
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Can you say where that is again?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, it's on page 2-24, and it's under the heading that says "At T start plus 7 minutes."
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
What was it?
I'm sorry; I got all of that, but didn't get what it was.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
It's change "20 seconds - 2 frames" to "1/15 second - 2 frames."
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, on page 2-36 [pause.] about 1/3 of the way down the page where it says "Configure lens f/8,1/30, 4" - We want to change that to "Configure lens 1/2 stop between f/5.6 and f/8, 1/15, and 4." [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, that's ½ stop between f/5.6 and f/8, and the shutter to 1/15?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's affirmative, Charlie.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: And on that same page, about 2/3 of the way down, under the step that says "Electrophoresis Power, On," we want to add a note to hold for instructions from MSFN.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, understand hold for instructions from MSFN.
Is that before the Power, On?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Negative.
That's immediately after Power, On, and I won't read you that instruction now; we'll wait until we get to that in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 16, Houston.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Go ahead, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Right, John.
This Verb 49 that's at seven hours in the Flight Plan, we want you to hold up on that so we can have a look at the attitude.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: 16, Houston.
We had a temporary very short loss of comm there.
Did you do anything on board to return comm?
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: We haven't touched a thing there, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Everything seems okay now.
It was very brief, but we lost it for a while.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
We didn't even hear any squelch or any of that noise...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: ...like you usually get when you lose comm.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Understand.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: 16, Houston.
The attitude in the Flight Plan for the Verb 49 is okay.
You can go ahead with it.
transcript.speaker: Peterson
